headline: Cyclosporiasis: FDA says Taylor Farms sample was a false positive — recall stands; NW Ohio surpasses 2,000 cases, Lucas County 967 (July 20)
paragraphs: The Toledo-Lucas County Health Department confirmed on June 30 the first cases of cyclosporiasis — a diarrheal intestinal illness caused by the parasite Cyclospora cayetanensis — in Lucas County residents, marking the Toledo metro's entry into an outbreak spreading across northwest Ohio and southeast Michigan. By July 8, Lucas County was leading all Ohio counties in confirmed cases; The Blade reported at least 65 Lucas County residents ill as of July 1, with case counts climbing steadily into early July. The Ohio Department of Health (ODH) issued a formal advisory on July 8, with ODH Director Dr. Keith Vanderhoff urging precautions and noting cyclosporiasis requires antibiotic treatment not resolved by over-the-counter antidiarrheal medications. CNBC reported July 13 that the national outbreak has surpassed 1,000 confirmed cases, with the Midwest — and northwest Ohio in particular — disproportionately represented. NW Ohio total cases exceeded 600 as of mid-July.

No specific food item or venue has been publicly identified as the source of the Lucas County outbreak as of this brief. The Toledo-Lucas County Health Department, Monroe County Health Department in Michigan, Ohio Department of Health, and federal agencies are jointly investigating, with patient interviews focused on grocery and dining locations in the 2–14 days before symptom onset. Past national cyclosporiasis outbreaks have been linked to contaminated fresh produce. Symptoms include watery diarrhea, stomach cramping, bloating, fatigue, and nausea; onset typically follows ingestion by 2 to 14 days. The Toledo-Lucas County Health Department has published updated case counts and guidance at lucascountyhealth.com/cyclo.

As of July 14-15, the 2026 outbreak has become the worst year on record for U.S. cyclosporiasis, surpassing the prior record of approximately 4,700 confirmed cases set in 2019. Cases have been confirmed in at least 31 states. Michigan's chief medical executive Dr. Natasha Bagdasarian said data from more than 1,000 patient interviews consistently implicates lettuce or salad greens as the exposure vehicle. Taco Bell has emerged as a focus of the traceback investigation: the Washington Post reported July 14 that authorities are actively investigating Taco Bell and lettuce as the outbreak source. Yum Brands confirmed to CNBC that Taco Bell voluntarily pulled select produce items — including lettuce, guacamole, pico de gallo, and cilantro onion — at select Detroit-area locations. Taco Bell stated there is 'no confirmed link' to the outbreak; no FDA recall has been issued naming the chain as of this brief. The CDC's official outbreak page continues to list the food source as 'unknown.' No specific Toledo restaurant or grocery supplier has been named in connection with the Lucas County case cluster.

As of July 15-16, northwest Ohio confirmed cases reached 1,199 — up 80 in a single day from July 14's count of 1,119, per The Blade's July 15 reporting. Lucas County's confirmed case total reached 682, rising from 531 on July 13 to 638 on July 14 and 682 on July 15, according to the Toledo-Lucas County Health Department's newly launched public dashboard at lucascountyhealth.com/cyclo, which updates weekday case counts at approximately noon. The Blade reported July 14 that northwest Ohio had officially exceeded 1,000 confirmed cases — a regional milestone. Wood County health officials also announced a local case tally, extending the outbreak's confirmed geographic reach within northwest Ohio. Nationally, NBC News reported that the CDC now counts nearly 7,000 suspected cases — 1,645 laboratory-confirmed plus approximately 5,100 under investigation — roughly 1.5 times the prior U.S. record of 4,700. No FDA recall has been issued as of this brief; the CDC's outbreak page continues to list the food source as 'unknown'; no Toledo restaurant or grocery supplier has been named. The Glass City JazzFest at Glass City Metropark is now fewer than 30 days away.

On July 16, federal investigators publicly identified the outbreak source for the first time. The CDC and FDA linked the outbreak to shredded iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms — a large California-based agricultural company — to Taco Bell restaurants in five states: Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and West Virginia. The Washington Post, CNN, and Bloomberg Law all reported July 16 that investigators had traced contaminated lettuce to a single supplier shipping from Mexico. Taco Bell announced it is removing lettuce from its U.S. locations nationwide indefinitely and will replace it with lettuce from alternative sources; notices at some Detroit-area Taco Bell restaurants had already informed customers the chain was 'currently unable to sell' lettuce, pico de gallo, guacamole, and cilantro onion. No FDA recall has been issued as of this brief — investigators describe Taylor Farms lettuce as a 'potential source' rather than a confirmed causal link; the CDC's official outbreak page updated its title to 'Cyclospora Outbreak Linked to Shredded Iceberg Lettuce Served at Taco Bell in 5 States.' Michigan's case total has surpassed 4,300 — with at least 100 hospitalizations — concentrated in southeast Michigan, where the Detroit metro carries the heaviest case load. NW Ohio cases reached 1,409 as of The Blade's July 16 reporting, with Lucas County at 750 — both up significantly from the July 15 count of 1,199 and 682, respectively. Nationally, 141 hospitalizations and nearly 7,000 confirmed or under-investigation cases have been recorded.

On July 17, Taylor Farms de Mexico formally announced a voluntary recall of all iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico — the first official recall of the 2026 outbreak. The FDA simultaneously published a formal advisory: 'Do not eat shredded iceberg lettuce from Mexico served at Taco Bell locations in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and West Virginia.' Taylor Farms stated the recall was prompted by information provided by the FDA. Taco Bell confirmed it 'has completed removal of affected Taylor Farms lettuce from our restaurants' and that 'the affected ingredient has been removed from our supply chain nationwide.' The Ohio Department of Health reported July 17 that Ohio has reached 1,316 confirmed cyclosporiasis cases — the state total topping 1,000 for the first time — with 96 hospitalizations statewide. ODH's confirmed count for Lucas County stands at 278. That figure is substantially lower than the 750 reported by the Toledo-Lucas County Health Department's dashboard at lucascountyhealth.com/cyclo, which tracks all reported and pending laboratory cases including those under investigation; the ODH figure reflects only lab-confirmed results in its system. The Toledo-Lucas County Health Department separately launched a survey — administered through the Ohio Department of Health — for any Lucas County resident who has tested positive for cyclosporiasis, to aid the investigation. With the supply chain exposure severed by Taco Bell's nationwide lettuce removal and the Taylor Farms recall, public health officials expect the incubation period (2–14 days) to determine when new cases stop appearing; any new cases arising after July 17 would reflect exposures prior to the recall.

Also on July 17, Michigan's cyclosporiasis case total surpassed 5,000 — up from 4,312 on July 16 — according to the Detroit News, making Michigan the most heavily affected state in the 2026 outbreak. Cases remain concentrated in southeast Michigan. July 19 is day two of the post-recall surveillance window. The Toledo-Lucas County Health Department's dashboard at lucascountyhealth.com/cyclo updates weekdays at approximately noon; no new case count is expected until Monday, July 20. The last published Lucas County total stands at 750 (NW Ohio: 1,409) as of July 16. Public health officials expect Cyclospora's 2–14-day incubation window to govern when new-exposure cases stop appearing; any case arising after July 17 reflects exposure prior to the recall. The Glass City JazzFest at Glass City Metropark is now 27 days away (August 15).

July 20 marks day three of the post-recall surveillance window and the first scheduled weekday case-count update from the Toledo-Lucas County Health Department's dashboard at lucascountyhealth.com/cyclo since the Taylor Farms voluntary recall was announced July 17. The dashboard updates at approximately noon on weekdays; as of this brief's publication, no confirmed updated count had appeared in available sources — the last published figures stand at Lucas County: 750 and NW Ohio: 1,409, as of July 16. Epidemiologists note that Cyclospora's 2–14-day incubation period means cases appearing this week may still reflect exposures predating the July 17 supply chain severance; a meaningful deceleration in daily new cases — if it materializes in today's and subsequent dashboard updates — will be the earliest empirical signal that removing Taylor Farms lettuce from Taco Bell's supply chain is working. The Glass City JazzFest at Glass City Metropark is now 26 days away (August 15).

Northwest Ohio surpassed 2,000 cyclosporiasis cases over the weekend. The Blade reported July 20 that the regional total reached 2,029 — up from 1,673 on Friday, July 17, the same day the Taylor Farms recall was announced — with Lucas County's confirmed case count rising to 967, up from 750 on July 16. The continued surge in weekend case counts confirms that Cyclospora's 2–14-day incubation period is masking any near-term signal from the supply chain intervention: cases appearing this week still reflect exposures prior to the July 17 recall. A meaningful plateau or deceleration signal is not expected until late July at the earliest.

On July 20, the FDA disclosed a significant complication in the investigation: the laboratory sample of Taylor Farms lettuce that tested positive for Cyclospora — the physical evidence cited when investigators linked the outbreak to Taylor Farms de Mexico and prompted the July 17 voluntary recall — was a false positive. CNBC, NBC News, and the Washington Post all reported the FDA's finding; the agency stated that 'due to the complexity in detection of Cyclospora, FDA laboratory experts re-reviewed the sample results and have concluded that the finding does not represent true amplification and should be considered a false positive.' As of the FDA's July 20 statement, there are no confirmed positive laboratory samples linking Taylor Farms lettuce to Cyclospora. Crucially, the Taylor Farms voluntary recall remains in effect: Taylor Fresh Foods said the recall was kept in place 'out of an abundance of caution.' The FDA has not reversed its conclusion that Taylor Farms is linked to the outbreak — the epidemiological case (patient interview data consistently pointing to Taco Bell lettuce) stands even without a confirmed laboratory sample. The false positive means the physical evidence underpinning the recall has been retracted; the investigation continues on epidemiological grounds alone. July 21 is day four of the post-recall surveillance window; the TLCHD dashboard is expected to update at approximately noon.
why_it_matters: The identification of Taylor Farms lettuce at Taco Bell as the outbreak source is a structurally significant development, but its implications for Toledo's summer economy are mixed. On one hand, a named food vehicle and chain — rather than an open-ended investigation — allows the exposure pathway to be severed: Taco Bell's nationwide lettuce removal is the lever. On the other, the outbreak has already become the worst on record nationally and Lucas County remains the leading Ohio county by case count. If the Taylor Farms/Taco Bell supply chain is the primary source, Lucas County's outsized case burden (750 of 1,409 NW Ohio cases — over 53%) may reflect the density of Taco Bell locations relative to population, not a separate localized source. The question that matters most for Toledo's summer economic calendar — will case counts fall now that the supply chain is being addressed? — will take 2–4 weeks to answer (Cyclospora's incubation period is 2–14 days). The Glass City JazzFest on August 15 remains fewer than 30 days away. For Toledo's restaurants and food-service sector: no Toledo establishment has been named in the investigation, and the identified exposure vehicle is a national fast-food chain — not a downtown restaurant or local supplier.
